title: Euler Topology in Superconducting Honeycomb Lattices
authors: Rasoul Ghadimi, Chiranjit Mondal, Bohm-Jung Yang
abstract: Electronic bands in systems with space-time inversion (IST) symmetry can host nontrivial Euler topology. Here, we investigate the band topology of IST-symmetric superconducting honeycomb lattices and demonstrate that s-wave spin-singlet (SWSS) and f-wave spin-triplet (FWST) superconducting pairings give rise to valley-Euler and Euler superconductors, respectively. We find that Euler topology in both pairing states gives rise to mirror-symmetry-protected helical domain-wall modes. Furthermore, we show that Euler topology in the FWST state induces non-Abelian braiding of Dirac nodes in momentum space when anisotropic hopping is introduced. Our work establishes superconducting electronic instabilities as a natural route to realizing nontrivial Euler band topology in Dirac materials.
summary: This paper shows how superconductivity in honeycomb lattices can create exotic topological phases known as Euler and valley-Euler superconductors. It reveals that these phases can host protected edge modes and exhibit non-Abelian braiding of Dirac nodes in momentum space. This provides a new route to realizing complex topological physics in Dirac materials.
main_problem: The study investigates the realization of nontrivial Euler band topology through superconducting electronic instabilities in Dirac materials, specifically honeycomb lattices.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that f-wave spin-triplet pairing leads to an Euler superconductor with non-Abelian braiding of Dirac nodes, while s-wave spin-singlet pairing results in a valley-Euler superconductor; both host mirror-symmetry-protected helical domain-wall modes.
method: The researchers use a Bogoliubov-de Gennes (BdG) framework to calculate topological invariants, specifically the Euler class and patch Euler class, and perform numerical real-space solutions of the BdG equation.
model_or_system: The system consists of monolayer honeycomb lattices (like graphene) with space-time inversion symmetry, modeled using a tight-binding Hamiltonian with nearest and next-nearest neighbor hopping and superconducting pairing potentials (SWSS and FWST).
key_observables: Euler class (chi), patch Euler class, energy dispersion, helical domain-wall modes, and Dirac node braiding.
important_parameters: Chemical potential (mu), hopping amplitudes (t1, t2), anisotropy parameter (alpha), and pairing symmetries (s-wave spin-singlet and f-wave spin-triplet).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the presence of space-time inversion (IST) symmetry and focuses on the regime of superconducting electronic instabilities.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the lattice structure, energy dispersion, and pairing potential distribution; Fig 2 illustrates the domain-wall setup and the energy spectra of helical modes; Fig 3 demonstrates the mechanism of Dirac node annihilation in the SWSS phase.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical system and symmetry, defines the superconducting models (SWSS and FWST), calculates topological invariants (Euler class), analyzes domain-wall modes, and explores the non-Abelian braiding of Dirac nodes under anisotropy.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Euler superconductor (ES) and valley-Euler superconduc- tor (VES) in honeycomb lattices. (a) Structure of honeycomb lat- tice and hopping parameters t1 (red), αt1 (green), and t2 (blue). (b) Normal-state energy dispersion of honeycomb lattice along the high- symmetry directions with t2 = 0.1t1 and α = 1. (c) Distribution of f-wave pairing potential in momentum space, where the first Bril- louin zone is shown by a dashed honeycomb. (d) BdG energy disper- sion in the zero-pairing limit for µ = 0.5t1 [blue dashed line in (b)]. (e, f) Schematic BdG energy dispersion, assuming (e) s-wave spin- singlet (SWSS) and (f) f-wave spin-triplet (FWST) pairing, where ± shows the relative patch...

FIG. 2. (a) Domain-wall setup, where the sign of the pairing poten- tial flips across the domain wall. The domain-wall energy spectrum is plotted for (b) SWSS [µ = 0.3t1, t2 = 0, d0 s = 0.05t1], (c) FWST [µ = 0.3t1, t2 = 0, d0 f = 0.01t1], and for (d) T2DEG [µ = −3.25t1]. In the energy spectra, the red and blue denote the expectation value of the domain-wall mirror symmetry MDW for each energy state. In SWSS and FWST, the bands with opposite eigenval- ues cross and form helical domain-wall energy states. In contrast, for the T2DEG, the bands with opposite mirror eigenvalues avoid cross- ing.

FIG. 3. (a) Normal-state energy dispersion of the honeycomb lattice for different anisotropic hopping strengths α. In the normal state, the two Dirac nodes merge and annihilate each other as α increases. (b–c) Evolution of nodal points of the BdG Hamiltonian (t2 = 0, µ = 0.3t1, with ds = 0.1t1 for SWSS and df = 0.1t1 for FWST) as a function of α for (b) SWSS and (c) FWST. The nodal points between the lower two bands are shown in blue, while those for the middle-band (pairing nodes) are shown in green. The normal-state Fermi contours are indicated in gray.
